aliases: Fried Gram Flour Ball Yogurt Dip

english: Fried Gram Flour Ball Yogurt Dip

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general raita class; no specific boondi raita entry exists. Closest defensible parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists for boondi raita specifically. A0CKP (plain yoghurt) used as the closest parent base matrix anchor; boondi (fried gram flour balls) are an added component not captured in this code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for boondi raita or raita as a food concept.

allergens: gluten (gram flour / besan may contain traces)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Boondi Raita is approximately 150 g (one small bowl), commonly served as a side dish alongside rice or roti at lunch or dinner.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single ICMR-NIN entry exists for boondi raita as a composite dish. Values are derived by combining ICMR-NIN data for plain yogurt (dahi) and fried gram flour (besan) boondi in approximate proportions typical of the dish (roughly 75% yogurt, 25% boondi by weight), with seasoning contributions to sodium. Core macros and micronutrients are consistent with ICMR-NIN component data. Cholesterol, saturated fat, and trans fat values are cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for plain whole-milk yogurt and fried chickpea-flour snacks.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — University of Sydney GI Database; analogous estimate for raita using plain yogurt (GI ~14–36) and fried chickpea-flour snacks (GI ~35–55)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for boondi raita. GI estimated at 45 (low) by weighted combination of plain yogurt GI (~14–36, University of Sydney GI Database) and fried gram flour boondi GI (~50–55, analogous to fried chickpea snacks in University of Sydney GI Database). Yogurt's low GI and protein content moderate the overall glycemic response. Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (45 × 10.0 × 1.5) / 100 = 6.75 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (225 mg/100 g) from added salt, black salt (kala namak), and seasoning; deep-fried boondi also contributes saturated fat
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Boondi Raita to small portions (≤100 g) and request reduced-salt preparation, as the combined sodium from black salt and regular salt can contribute significantly to daily sodium targets of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~45) moderated by yogurt protein and fat, but fried gram flour boondi adds refined carbohydrates and fat
type2_diabetes.guidance: Boondi Raita has a low GI (~45) and moderate carbohydrate content (10 g/100 g), making it acceptable in controlled portions (120–150 g) for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, though the fried boondi component should be monitored for caloric density.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (4.0 g/100 g) and trans fat (0.25 g/100 g) from deep-fried boondi; cholesterol (15 mg/100 g) from dairy
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume Boondi Raita in moderation due to its saturated fat and trans fat content from deep-frying; opting for baked or air-fried boondi and low-fat yogurt can reduce cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Yogurt provides probiotics which may support gut-immune axis, but deep-fried gram flour can be pro-inflammatory
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis may benefit from the probiotic content of yogurt in Boondi Raita, but should monitor personal tolerance to fried gram flour, which may exacerbate inflammation in some individuals.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Boondi Raita is a popular North Indian yogurt-based side dish (raita) made by combining boondi — small, crispy, deep-fried balls of gram flour (besan) — with whisked, seasoned plain yogurt (dahi). The boondi may be added directly for a crunchy texture or soaked briefly in water to achieve a softer, spongy consistency. The dish is seasoned with roasted cumin powder (jeera), red chili powder, black salt (kala namak), and regular salt, giving it a tangy, mildly spiced, and cooling flavour profile. Boondi Raita is a staple accompaniment in North Indian cuisine, commonly served alongside biryani, pulao, roti, and sabzi at lunch and dinner. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 140 kcal, 5 g protein, 9 g fat (of which 4 g is saturated), and 10 g carbohydrates, with 100 mg calcium from the yogurt base. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 45 (low), moderated by the protein and fat content of yogurt. The sodium content (225 mg/100 g) is elevated due to black salt and added seasoning, making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension. The dish is vegetarian and halal-suitable. It is best consumed fresh and chilled as a cooling counterpart to spicy dishes.

meal_context: cooling side dish for spicy meals

confidence_reason: No single ICMR-NIN entry exists for boondi raita as a composite dish. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN component data for plain yogurt and fried gram flour boondi in typical proportions. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database entries for yogurt and fried chickpea-flour snacks. Confidence is medium rather than high because no direct approved-source measurement of the composite dish was available.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for boondi raita as a composite dish is not reported in ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for this specific preparation. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the composite dish is not reported in available approved sources. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.25 g/100 g based on the fried boondi component; this is a low but non-zero value consistent with deep-frying in vegetable oil. All core macros (protein_g 5.0, fat_g 9.0, carbohydrate_g 10.0) are non-zero and nutritionally plausible for a yogurt-and-fried-gram-flour composite dish. Portions in the input contained duplicates and multiple is_default:true entries; deduplicated to a single default serving of 150 g (1 small bowl) for clinical consistency. GI glycemic load calculated as: (45 × 10.0 × 1.5) / 100 = 6.75 g. Iteration 3 fix: ontology.foodex2_match_type corrected from 'adapted' to 'closest_parent' to conform to the allowed enumeration ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none'] as required by g5_ontology_honesty.
